text: The crystal structure of the high mobility semimetal Cd3As2, which hosts electronic Dirac particles, has been re-examined by means of single crystal X-ray diffraction experiments, and found to be centrosymmetric in contrast with previous reports of a noncentrosymmetric structure, thus confirming the Pmn21 space group assignment (ref. [17]) with some small deviations and being in full agreement with the one recently reported by ref. [17]. In this centrosymmetric phase, Cd3As2 crystallizes in the tetragonal I4_1/acd space group with a = 12.633(3) Å and c = 25.427(7) Å and an antifluorite (M2X) type distorted structure. Such structure is composed of undistorted Cd6square2 (whereasquare2 stands for a tetrahedral vacancy) octamers which are placed along the c axis in a column and the + and - chiral columns are alternatively stacked along c. First principles band structure calculations based on this centrosymmetric structure shows that the inversion symmetry of the structure implies that the spin splitting of the bands does not occur and that the bands remain spin degenerate. As a result, a four-fold degenerate bulk Dirac point, which is similar to that of graphene, is found at the Fermi energy along the Γ-Z line in the Brillouin zone. Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) experiments, which were carried out on the (112) surface of a Cd3As2 single crystalline sample, have evidenced a 2 × 2 reconstruction on this surface and the morphology of a needle-like crystal growing along the [110] direction has been determined.
headline: Fully Human Written
